Name: dynamic_spectral_alignment_inference
Title: Dynamic Spectral Alignment for Latency-Aware Inference in Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: Dynamic spectral alignment of hidden state activations during inference can enforce strict latency budgets while preserving accuracy, by adapting computational pathways based on real-time spectral stability metrics.
Related Work: Existing work has explored spectral decomposition (e.g., Spectral Quantization Routing, Geometric Signal Separation) for inference optimization, but these approaches often rely on static or precomputed spectral properties. Recent work like Spectral Latent Steering and Geometric Interference Cancellation has introduced dynamic subspaces, but they focus on task-specific interference or latent steering rather than general-purpose inference optimization. Our proposal uniquely addresses real-time spectral alignment as a mechanism for latency-aware inference, bridging spectral theory with practical compute constraints.
Abstract: We propose Dynamic Spectral Alignment (DSA), a training-free framework for latency-aware neural network inference. DSA dynamically aligns hidden state activations with their spectral subspaces during inference, adapting computational pathways based on real-time spectral stability metrics. By performing on-the-fly singular value decomposition (SVD) of activations, DSA identifies dominant spectral directions and projects computations onto these subspaces, ensuring strict latency budgets while preserving accuracy. We introduce a spectral stability metric derived from the condition number of activation covariances, which serves as a proxy for computational difficulty. Tokens residing in well-conditioned subspaces are routed through fewer layers or lower-precision computations, while ill-conditioned tokens receive full computational resources. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks, DSA achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic baselines. Our framework provides a mathematically transparent, reproducible pathway for efficient inference under natural distribution shifts, bridging foundational spectral theory with practical system-scale optimization.
Experiments: Benchmark Evaluation: Tasks include image classification (CIFAR-10, ImageNet), text generation (GPT-2), and mathematical reasoning (GSM8K). Baselines include static precision allocation, heuristic mixed-precision methods, and existing spectral decomposition frameworks. Metrics: Accuracy, latency (ms), memory usage, and spectral stability (condition number).

Ablation Studies: Impact of spectral stability thresholds on accuracy and latency. Sensitivity to different SVD approximation methods (randomized vs. exact). Effect of dynamic routing policies (layer skipping, precision switching).
Robustness Evaluation: Performance under distributional shifts (WILDS benchmarks). Stability under adversarial perturbations (FGSM, PGD).
Scalability Analysis: Evaluation on larger models (BERT, ResNet-50) and multi-GPU setups. Comparison of inference latency and memory usage across hardware configurations.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Computational Overhead: On-the-fly SVD may introduce latency overhead for very large models.
Approximation Errors: Randomized SVD approximations may lead to suboptimal spectral alignments.
Generalization: The framework may require task-specific tuning for optimal performance.
Hardware Constraints: Limited scalability on memory-constrained devices due to intermediate activation storage.
